prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following feedback. Write the full script in English, approximately 10 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Topic
Based on professional feedback and extracted issues (see Feedback Context below).

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Feedback Context
## Feedback
Subject: Classroom observations — Sam
Date: 2024-03-10
Content: Sam struggles to wait his turn during group activities and often blurts out answers. He becomes frustrated quickly when he makes mistakes and has occasionally thrown materials. He responds very well to one-on-one adult attention and is kind-hearted with peers.
Tags: impulse-control, frustration-tolerance, classroom-behavior

## Extracted Issues (2)
- **Impulse Control Difficulties** [MODERATE/BEHAVIORAL]: Difficulty waiting turns and inhibiting verbal responses in group settings.
  - Recommendation: Self-monitoring strategies
  - Recommendation: Visual cues for turn-taking
- **Low Frustration Tolerance** [MODERATE/EMOTIONAL]: Strong emotional reactions to mistakes; difficulty self-regulating after errors.
  - Recommendation: Mistake normalization
  - Recommendation: Breathing techniques for frustration

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Emotion regulation training for impulse control in young children" (2023)
   Key findings: Stop-signal training and emotion labeling reduced disruptive behavior by 35% in ages 6-9
   Therapeutic techniques: Turtle technique; emotion labeling; stop-breathe-think sequence

2. "Self-regulation interventions for frustration tolerance in middle childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Mindful breathing and reframing mistakes as learning reduced emotional outbursts
   Therapeutic techniques: Traffic light method; growth mindset reframing; 5-finger breathing

## LEGO Therapeutic Play (REQUIRED)
This session MUST integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity. This is NOT optional — LEGO play is a core modality for this age group:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- MUST include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional for the listener: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal
- Dedicate at least 30% of the session time to LEGO-based activities

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- CRITICAL: Every breathing exercise MUST have explicit counted timing. NEVER write just "take a deep breath". ALWAYS write: "Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five..." If you mention breathing at all, include the numbered counts.
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- Address the specific issues identified in the feedback, providing practical strategies for each
- Validate the observations from the professional who provided the feedback
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- LEGO play is REQUIRED for this session — include guided LEGO building activities as described in the LEGO Therapeutic Play section above
- Target duration: 10 minutes (approximately 1200 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam... I'm really glad you're here with me today... I heard you've been having some big feelings at school... and that's totally okay... Lots of kids have big feelings sometimes... Today, we're going to play a game and build something cool together... It's all about being the boss of your body and your words... Are you ready? Good.

You know, Sam... sometimes our bodies want to move really fast... Our words want to jump right out of our mouths... It's like having a super speedy race car inside... It just wants to GO... And sometimes, when we make a little mistake, it can feel really, really hot inside... like a volcano getting ready to pop... I get that... The grown-ups who see you at school told me something really nice, too... They said you have a very kind heart... And you are really good at listening when it's just you and one grown-up... That's awesome, Sam... So today, we're going to use that super listening power.

First, let's play a game with our bodies... It's called the Turtle Technique... When a turtle gets scared or needs to slow down, what does it do? That's right... it goes inside its shell... We can do that too... Let's try it together... When I say "turtle," I want you to pull your shoulders up to your ears... Tuck your chin down a little... and wrap your arms around yourself like a big hug... Go ahead and try it now... Turtle... Good job, Sam... You're in your safe shell... In here, we can take a quiet moment... We can take a big, slow breath.

Let's do that breath together now... From inside your turtle shell... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Good... Let's do that one more time... Breathe in... two... three... four... And let it go... two... three... four... five... Perfect... You can relax your arms now... That is your superpower pause... You can use that anytime... When you feel your race car body wanting to zoom... or your words wanting to jump out... just think... turtle... shell... breath... You are the boss.

Now, Sam, we're going to add some LEGO magic... If you have some LEGO bricks near you, grab a few now... Any colors or shapes you like... If you don't have any, that's totally fine... We can imagine building together... Got your bricks or your imagination ready? Great.

We're going to build something called a Stop and Think Bridge... First, find one brick... This brick is red... A red brick is like our "STOP" signal... It's like a red traffic light... Place your red brick down in front of you... Good... This red brick reminds us to freeze... just like a turtle... It means... stop... body... stop... words...

Now, Sam, find a yellow brick... A yellow brick is for "THINK" and "BREATHE"... It's like a yellow light that says "get ready"... Place your yellow brick right next to the red one... Good job... This yellow brick is for taking that calm breath in your shell... It's your moment to think... "What do I need right now?"

Finally, find a green brick... Green means "GO"... but a calm, careful go... Place your green brick next to the yellow one... Now you have a little bridge... Red... yellow... green... This green brick is for the kind action... Maybe it's raising your hand... Maybe it's trying again slowly... You built that, Sam... Your very own Stop and Think Bridge.

Sometimes we try things and they don't work the first time... The tower might fall over... That can feel so frustrating, can't it? Like that hot volcano feeling... Let's talk about mistakes, Sam... Mistakes are not bad... They are like secret teachers... Every time your LEGO tower falls, you learn a better way to build it next time... That's how we grow... Let's practice that now.

I want you to build a small tower... Just stack a few bricks up... Make it as tall as you want... Go ahead, I'll wait... ... ... Is it tall? Great... Now, I want you to gently knock it over... Whoops! It fell!... That was a mistake in our building game... How does your body feel? A little funny? That's okay.

Now, here is the magic part... We are going to breathe through the "whoops" feeling... Put your hand on your chest... Feel your heart beating... Let's cool down the volcano with our breath... Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Good... Now, let's build the tower again... But let's build it even better this time... Maybe use a bigger brick at the bottom... Try it... ... ... You see, Sam? The mistake helped you learn... You are a builder who learns... Every time something is hard, you get to learn and grow... That's called a growth mindset... It means your brain is getting stronger.

Let's use your LEGO bricks one more way... I want you to build a tiny "Calm Castle"... This is a special place in your mind... Pick bricks that feel calm to you... Maybe blue like a quiet sky... or green like soft grass... Build a little square... just a few bricks high... This is your castle where you can feel safe when feelings get big... Put a little space inside it... That space is for you... Go ahead and build it... ... ... Beautiful, Sam.

If you feel that hot, frustrated feeling at school... you can picture your Calm Castle... You can remember your turtle shell breath... And you can think about your red, yellow, and green bridge... Stop... Breathe and think... Then go calmly.

You did such amazing work today, Sam... You practiced being the boss of your race car body... You learned that mistakes help us grow... And you built a bridge and a castle with your own hands... That's a lot of great stuff.

Here's something you can try with a grown-up later... Show them your Stop and Think Bridge... Teach them the turtle technique... You can be the teacher... Remember, Sam, having big feelings means you have a big, kind heart... It just needs a little practice being the boss... Keep building, keep breathing, and keep being your awesome self... I'm so proud of you. If you ever need more help, you can always talk to a parent, a caregiver, or another grown-up you trust. Goodbye for now.
